macro_segments.title: Introduction to Makkot: Structure, Continuation from Sanhedrin, and Overview of the Tractate's Scope

micro_segments.title: Makkot as a Small Tractate: Three Chapters and Historical Organization
Makkot as Continuation of Sanhedrin: Evidence from Transition via False Witnesses
Conceptual Framework: From Capital Punishments to Other Judicial Functions
Structure of Remaining Chapters: Exile (Galus) and Lashes as Non-Monetary Punishments

macro_segments.title: The Challenge of Systematizing Exile: Arbitrariness, Formalism, and the Search for Underlying Logic in Cases of Unintentional Homicide

micro_segments.title: Opening Observation: Apparent Lack of Consistent Principles in Determining Eligibility for Exile (Galus)
The Doctrine of Yerida Letzorech Aliyah: Downward Motion for the Sake of Upward Motion in Chopping Wood
Rashi and Tosafot on Yerida Letzorech Aliyah: When Does a Rung Actually Fall, and What Constitutes Downward Motion?

macro_segments.title: The Axe Head Case (Nishmata Habarzel): Debate Between Rabbi and Sages on Transitive vs. Intransitive Readings of Scripture

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Two Interpretations of 'The Iron Was Let Loose from the Wood'—Iron Flies Off vs. Splinter Flies Off
Gemara: Rabbi's Argument from Linguistic Context—The Word 'Wood' Refers to Wood Being Chopped, Not the Handle
Rav's Resolution: Both Opinions Parse the Same Verse; Rabbi Reads Transitive Form via Contextual Clues (Tosafot's Approach)
The Dirt and Date Palm Case: Formal Similarity to Axe Head Debate—Using One Object to Make Another Fly Off
Kocho and Koach Kocho: Degrees of Removal from the Actor and Whether Splinters Constitute One or Two Degrees of Separation

macro_segments.title: Throwing Stones into Public Domain and the Role of Negligence and Right to Be Present

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Throwing Stones into Public Domain Results in Exile; Exception When Stone Leaves Hand Before Person Enters Path
Throwing into One's Own Courtyard: Right to Be There as Determinant of Exile Liability
Abba Shaul's Drasha: Discretionary Acts Only; Exclusion of Father Hitting Son, Teacher Hitting Student, and Beit Din Officer
Gemara on Public Domain: Negligence (Poshea) vs. Unforeseeability (Onus); the Case of the Dump Used at Night
Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov: Person Placing Themselves in Danger Exempts Actor—Formalistic Rather Than Fault-Based

macro_segments.title: Discretionary vs. Obligatory Acts: The Methurgeman Dispute and Clarification of When Exemptions Apply to Mitzvah Contexts

micro_segments.title: Challenge: If the Torah's Case is Discretionary Chopping, What About Chopping for Sukkah or Temple Purposes?
Rava's Defense: Means to a Mitzvah Are Not Mitzvot Per Se; Chopping is Never Intrinsically Obligatory (Yesh Eim L'Masoret)
Ravina's Counter-Challenge: If Means Count as Discretionary, Then Hitting One's Child Should Also Be Exempt—Yet It's Not
Rava's Troubled Response: Hitting a Child Is Intrinsically a Mitzvah (Yesser Bincha) Despite Appearing Merely a Means
Rava Retracts: The Distinction Is Necessity, Not Intrinsic Mitzvah Status—Means That Are Necessary Count as Mitzvah Acts
Parallel Sugya on Shabbat: Rabbi Ishmael on Discretionary Plowing and Harvesting; the Omer Harvest as Necessary Exception
Ravina's Challenge via Omer Case: How Can We Say Necessary Acts Count as Mitzvot When Omer Harvesting Overrides Shabbat Anyway?
Final Resolution: The Line Between Discretionary and Mitzvah Contexts Is Not Intrinsic Value but Necessity and Context—Hitting One's Child Is Necessary, Hence Mitzvah-Adjacent
